analogy: Think of "Rise of Nationalism in Europe" through the chapter's own opening images: 3 Nationalism in Europe In 1848, Frédéric Sorrieu, a French artist, prepared a series of four prints visualising his dream of a world made up of ‘democratic and social Republics’, as he called them. 1) of the series, shows the peoples of Europe and America... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: EVENTS AND PROCESSES, SECTION I, 1 The French Revolution and the Idea of the Nation, ICELAND. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Rise of Nationalism in Europe is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 3 Nationalism in Europe In 1848, Frédéric Sorrieu, a French artist, prepared a series of four prints visualising his dream of a world made up of ‘democratic and social Republics’, as he called them.

The text explains that 1) of the series, shows the peoples of Europe and America – men and women of all ages and social classes – marching in a long train, and offering homage to the statue of Liberty as they pass by it.

The text explains that as you would recall, artists of the time of the French Revolution personified Liberty as a female figure – here you can recognise the torch of Enlightenment she bears in one hand and the Charter of the Rights of Man in the other.

The text explains that on the earth in the foreground of the image lie the shattered remains of the symbols of absolutist institutions.

The text explains that in Sorrieu’s utopian vision, the peoples of the world are grouped as distinct nations, identified through their flags and national costume.

The text explains that leading the procession, way past the statue of Liberty, are the United States and Switzerland, which by this time were already nation-states.

The text explains that france, T h e R i s e o f N a t i o n a l i s m i n E u r o p e Fig.

The text explains that 1 — The Dream of Worldwide Democratic and Social Republics – The Pact Between Nations, a print prepared by Frédéric Sorrieu, 1848.

The text explains that chapter I The Rise of Nationalism in Europe New words Absolutist – Literally, a government or system of rule that has no restraints on the power exercised.

The text explains that in history, the term refers to a for m of monarchical government that was centralised, militarised and repressive Utopian – A vision of a society that is so ideal that it is unlikely to actually exist In what way do you think this print (Fig.

The text explains that india and the Contemporary World 4 identifiable by the revolutionary tricolour, has just reached the statue.

The text explains that she is followed by the peoples of Germany, bearing the black, red and gold flag.

The text explains that interestingly, at the time when Sorrieu created this image, the German peoples did not yet exist as a united nation – the flag they carry is an expression of liberal hopes in 1848 to unify the numerous German-speaking principalities into a nation-state under a democratic constitution.

The text explains that following the German peoples are the peoples of Austria, the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies, Lombardy, Poland, England, Ireland, Hungary and Russia.

The text explains that from the heavens above, Christ, saints and angels gaze upon the scene.

The text explains that they have been used by the artist to symbolise fraternity among the nations of the world.

The text explains that this chapter will deal with many of the issues visualised by Sorrieu in Fig.

The text explains that but a nation-state was one in which the majority of its citizens, and not only its rulers, came to develop a sense of common identity and shared history or descent.

The text explains that this chapter will look at the diverse processes through which nation-states and nationalism came into being in nineteenth-century Europe.

The text explains that a nation is therefore a large-scale solidarity … Its existence is a daily plebiscite … A province is its inhabitants; if anyone has the right to be consulted, it is the inhabitant.

The text explains that their existence is a guarantee of liberty, which would be lost if the world had only one law and only one master.’ Source Source A Summarise the attributes of a nation, as Renan understands them.

The text explains that discuss New words Plebiscite – A direct vote by which all the people of a region are asked to accept or reject a proposal

The text explains that the political and constitutional changes that came in the wake of the French Revolution led to the transfer of sovereignty from the monarchy to a body of French citizens.

The text explains that the ideas of la patrie (the fatherland) and le citoyen (the citizen) emphasised the notion of a united community enjoying equal rights under a constitution.

The text explains that a centralised administrative system was put in place and it formulated uniform laws for all citizens within its territory.

The text explains that internal customs duties and dues were abolished and a uniform system of weights and measures was adopted.

The text explains that through a return to monarchy Napoleon had, no doubt, destroyed democracy in France, but in the administrative field he had incorporated revolutionary principles in order to make the whole system more rational and efficient.

The text explains that the Civil Code of 1804 – usually known as the Napoleonic Code – did away with all privileges based on birth, established equality before the law and secured the right to property.

The text explains that in the Dutch Republic, in Switzerland, in Italy and Germany, Napoleon simplified administrative divisions, abolished the feudal system and freed peasants from serfdom and manorial dues.

The text explains that transport and communication systems were improved.

The text explains that they spoke French for purposes of diplomacy and in high society.

The text explains that 1848 Revolutions in Europe; artisans, industrial workers and peasants revolt against economic hardships; middle classes demand constitutions and representative governments; Italians, Germans, Magyars, Poles, Czechs, etc. demand nation-states.

The text explains that politically, it emphasised the concept of government by consent.

The text explains that since the French Revolution, liberalism had stood for the end of autocracy and clerical privileges, a constitution and representative government through parliament.

The text explains that you will recall that in revolutionary France, which marked the first political experiment in liberal democracy, the right to vote and to get elected was granted exclusively to property-owning men.

The text explains that men without property and all women were excluded from political rights.

The text explains that throughout the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries women and non-propertied men organised opposition movements demanding equal political rights.

The text explains that napoleon’s administrative measures had created out of New words Suffrage – The right to vote

The text explains that as each region had its own system of weights and measures, this involved time-consuming calculation.

The text explains that a wave of economic nationalism strengthened the wider nationalist sentiments growing at the time.

The text explains that 2.3 A New Conservatism after 1815 Following the defeat of Napoleon in 1815, European governments were driven by a spirit of conservatism.

The text explains that conservatives believed that established, traditional institutions of state and society – like the monarchy, the Church, social hierarchies, property and the family – should be preserved.

The text explains that most conservatives, however, did not propose a return to the society of pre-revolutionary days.

The text explains that a modern army, an efficient bureaucracy, a dynamic economy, the abolition of feudalism and serfdom could strengthen the autocratic monarchies of Europe.

The text explains that the delegates Economists began to think in terms of the national economy.

The text explains that the German people have realised that a free economic system is the only means to engender national feeling.’ Source Source B Describe the political ends that List hopes to achieve through economic measures.

The text explains that how was the history of nationalism in Britain unlike the rest of Europe?

The text explains that why did nationalist tensions emerge in the Balkans?

The text explains that find out more about nationalist symbols in countries outside Europe.

The text explains that for one or two countries, collect examples of pictures, posters or music that are symbols of nationalism.

The text explains that how are these different from European examples?

The text explains that write a note on: a) Guiseppe Mazzini b) Count Camillo de Cavour c) The Greek war of independence d) Frankfurt parliament e) The role of women in nationalist struggles 2.

Source: NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II, Chapter 1: Rise of Nationalism in Europe
